features.summary: Chevron reported Q4 2024 earnings of $3.2 billion ($1.84 per share) with adjusted earnings of $3.6 billion ($2.06 per share). The company achieved record global and US production, with Permian production growing 18% year-over-year. Key milestones included completing PDC Energy integration, achieving first oil at FGP at TCO, and starting up Anchor and Whale projects in the Gulf of Mexico. Chevron returned a record $27 billion to shareholders through dividends and buybacks, announced a 5% dividend increase marking 38 consecutive years of increases, and maintained strong balance sheet with 10% net debt ratio.
features.management_outlook: Management expects industry-leading free cash flow growth with $10 billion additional annual free cash flow by 2026, driven by advantaged upstream assets. Production is expected to grow around 6% annually through 2026, with growth weighted toward second half of 2025 as key projects ramp up. The company plans to moderate Permian growth and CapEx to drive predictable free cash flow generation, targeting 1 million boe/d in 2025. Organic CapEx is expected to remain within $14-16 billion guidance range, with affiliate CapEx trending down as major projects complete.
features.risks: The company faces margin pressure in downstream and chemicals businesses due to softer refining margins and timing effects. Geopolitical risks remain in key operating regions including Kazakhstan (TCO concession extension negotiations), Eastern Mediterranean, and Venezuela (sanctions compliance). Execution risks exist with major project ramp-ups at FGP, Anchor, and Whale, requiring safe and reliable operations in challenging technical environments. Policy and regulatory changes could impact operations, particularly regarding permitting and environmental regulations.
features.key_drivers: Major production growth drivers include FGP at TCO reaching 1 million boe/d capacity within three months, Gulf of Mexico projects (Anchor, Whale ramping, Ballymore mid-2025), and Permian reaching 1 million boe/d in 2025. The company is targeting $2-3 billion in structural cost reductions by end-2026 through asset sales, technology deployment, and operational efficiency improvements. New energy initiatives include renewable diesel expansion, ACES hydrogen project startup, and new behind-the-meter power business targeting data center demand with seven GE Vernova turbines secured for delivery starting late 2026.
